days.title: Greetings and first contact

vocab.english: for a long time, long ago
vocab.exampleEn: We haven't seen each other for a long time.
He is an old acquaintance of mine.

vocab.example: Искам да се запозная с теб.
vocab.exampleEn: I want to get acquainted with you.

grammar.title: Opening a conversation
grammar.explanation: Small talk in Bulgarian starts with a greeting plus a question about the other person. Use 'Как си?' with friends and family, but 'Как сте?' with strangers, older people, or a group. The reply almost always uses the verb 'to be': 'Добре съм' (I am fine), 'Чудесно съм' (I am great). Remember Bulgarian really has a present-tense 'to be' (съм, си, е, сме, сте, са), unlike Russian. To say you are glad, use 'Радвам се' followed by 'да' + present tense: 'Радвам се да те видя' (I'm glad to see you), because there is no infinitive.

examples.russian: Радвам се да се запозная с вас.

reading.title: Стара позната
reading.text: — Здравей! Не сме се виждали отдавна. Как си? — Добре съм, благодаря. Радвам се да те видя!
reading.translation: — Hi! We haven't seen each other for a long time. How are you? — I'm fine, thank you. I'm glad to see you!

days.speakingPrompt: Greet an old acquaintance you haven't seen in a while. Ask how they are and say you are glad to see them, using both 'Как си?' and 'Радвам се'.

days.title: Talking about the weather

vocab.exampleEn: It's been raining since the morning.

vocab.example: Има само един облак на небето.
vocab.exampleEn: There is only one cloud in the sky.

vocab.example: Вали цял ден. Какво ужасно време!
vocab.exampleEn: It's been raining all day. What awful weather!

grammar.title: Weather as small talk
grammar.explanation: Weather is the safest small-talk topic. Bulgarian uses the verb 'to be' (е) with a neuter adjective for general states: 'Топло е' (It's warm), 'Студено е' (It's cold), 'Слънчево е' (It's sunny). Note the neuter -о ending on these adjectives because they describe an impersonal 'it'. For rain or snow use the special verb 'вали': 'Вали (дъжд)' = it's raining, 'Вали сняг' = it's snowing. The definite article is a suffix: време → времето (the weather). To exclaim, use 'Какво + adjective + време!'.

reading.title: Навън
reading.text: — Какво ужасно време! Вали от сутринта. — Да, студено е. Утре ще бъде слънчево, надявам се.
reading.translation: — What awful weather! It's been raining since the morning. — Yes, it's cold. Tomorrow it will be sunny, I hope.

days.speakingPrompt: Make small talk about today's weather. Say whether it is sunny, warm, cold, or raining, and add an exclamation like 'Какво хубаво/ужасно време!'.

days.title: Polite phrases and reactions

vocab.exampleEn: I'm a little late. — No problem!

vocab.example: За съжаление не мога да дойда.
Може ли да помогна? — Разбира се!
Всичко е наред, не се притеснявай.
И ти си тук? Какво съвпадение!
vocab.exampleEn: You're here too? What a coincidence!

grammar.title: Ready-made reactions
grammar.explanation: Conversation flows when you have set reactions ready. Many are fixed phrases you simply memorize: 'Разбира се' (of course), 'Няма проблем' (no problem), 'За съжаление' (unfortunately), 'Жалко' (too bad). Some agree with the speaker's gender: a man says 'Съгласен съм', a woman says 'Съгласна съм' (I agree), and 'Прав си' / 'Права си' (you're right). To reassure, use the negative command 'Не се притеснявай' (don't worry, informal) or 'Не се притеснявайте' (formal/plural). Notice 'се' stays with the verb.

examples.russian: Може ли да попитам нещо? — Разбира се.
examples.english: May I ask something? — Of course.
Unfortunately I don't have time today.

reading.title: Малка молба
reading.text: — Може ли да ми помогнеш? — Разбира се, няма проблем! — Благодаря ти много. — Не се притеснявай.
reading.translation: — Can you help me? — Of course, no problem! — Thank you very much. — Don't worry about it.

days.speakingPrompt: A friend asks you for a small favor. Respond using at least three set reactions: agree ('Разбира се'), reassure ('Няма проблем' / 'Не се притеснявай'), and react to news ('Жалко' or 'Какво съвпадение!').

days.title: Asking about someone's life

vocab.english: What are you doing? / How's it going?
vocab.exampleEn: Hey, what are you up to?

grammar.title: Catching up
grammar.explanation: After the greeting, Bulgarians ask open questions like 'Какво ново?' (What's new?) or 'Какво правиш?' (What are you doing / How's it going?). Typical low-key answers are 'Нищо особено' (nothing special) and 'Както обикновено' (as usual). To ask about specific areas of life, add the definite-article suffix to the noun: 'Как е семейството?' (How is the family?), 'Как върви работата?' (How is work going?). Adjectives describing you agree with gender: a man is 'зает', a woman is 'заета' (busy). There are no noun cases — relations come from word order and prepositions like 'при' (at, with): 'Какво ново при теб?'.

examples.russian: Нищо особено, всичко е както обикновено.

reading.title: Среща на улицата
reading.text: — Какво ново при теб? — Нищо особено, както обикновено. Много съм зает с работата. — А семейството? — Всички са добре.
reading.translation: — What's new with you? — Nothing special, as usual. I'm very busy with work. — And the family? — Everyone is well.

days.speakingPrompt: Run into a friend and catch up. Ask 'Какво ново?', then answer about your own week (use 'зает/заета', 'както обикновено'), and ask about their family and work.

days.title: Making plans and invitations

vocab.exampleEn: Maybe I'll be a little late.

vocab.example: Искаш ли да се видим довечера?
vocab.exampleEn: Do you want to meet up this evening?

grammar.title: Suggesting and accepting
grammar.explanation: To suggest doing something together, use 'Хайде да' + present tense: 'Хайде да пием кафе' (Let's have coffee), 'Хайде да се видим' (Let's meet). Remember there is no infinitive, so 'да' + a conjugated verb does the job. To check availability, ask 'Имаш ли време?' (informal) or 'Имате ли време?' (formal). The particle 'ли' makes a yes/no question and goes after the verb. Accept warmly with 'С удоволствие!' or 'Звучи добре'; soften or hesitate with 'Може би'. Ask for details with 'Кога?' (when) and 'Къде?' (where).

examples.english: Do you have time this evening?

examples.russian: Хайде да се видим в кафенето.

reading.title: Покана за кафе
reading.text: — Имаш ли време утре? Хайде да пием кафе. — С удоволствие! Кога? — В пет часа? — Звучи добре.
reading.translation: — Do you have time tomorrow? Let's have a coffee. — With pleasure! When? — At five o'clock? — Sounds good.

days.speakingPrompt: Invite a friend to do something with you. Ask if they have time, suggest an activity with 'Хайде да...', accept their reply, and agree on a time ('Кога?', 'Звучи добре').

days.title: Saying goodbye

vocab.exampleEn: It got late, I have to go.

vocab.example: Беше ми приятно да си поговорим.

vocab.exampleEn: See you soon, give me a call!
Say hi to the family from me.

grammar.title: Closing a conversation; putting it all together
grammar.explanation: Bulgarians signal the end of a chat before the final 'чао' or 'довиждане'. Announce you must leave with 'Трябва да...' + present tense: 'Трябва да тръгвам' (I have to go) — again 'да' replaces the missing infinitive. Then wrap up warmly: 'Беше ми приятно' (it was nice), 'Пази се' / 'Пазете се' (take care), 'До скоро' (see you soon), 'Приятен ден' (have a nice day). Send regards with 'Поздрави + (на) someone': 'Поздрави семейството' (say hi to the family) — note the definite-article suffix on the noun. Putting the week together: greet → small talk (weather, what's new) → make plans → close politely. These set phrases let you carry a whole everyday conversation from start to finish.

examples.english: I have to go, it got late.
See you soon and say hi to the family!

reading.title: Раздяла
reading.text: — Стана късно, трябва да тръгвам. — Добре. Беше ми приятно! — На мен също. Поздрави семейството и се обади. — До скоро, пази се!
reading.translation: — It got late, I have to go. — Okay. It was nice! — Likewise. Say hi to the family and give me a call. — See you soon, take care!

days.speakingPrompt: Wrap up a conversation politely. Say you have to go, say it was nice, send regards to someone's family, and finish with 'До скоро' and 'Пази се'.

days.title: Review: A whole everyday conversation

grammar.title: Recap: from hello to goodbye
grammar.explanation: This week you built complete everyday conversations from set phrases. Recap the flow: (1) Open with 'Как си?' / 'Как сте?' and reply with the verb 'to be' — 'Добре съм'. (2) Small talk about the weather using 'е' + neuter adjective ('Топло е') or 'вали' for rain. (3) Catch up with 'Какво ново?', answering 'Нищо особено' / 'Както обикновено', and ask 'Как е семейството?' using the article suffix. (4) React with fixed phrases: 'Разбира се', 'За съжаление', 'Жалко', gender-agreeing 'Съгласен/Съгласна съм'. (5) Make plans with 'Хайде да' + present and 'Имаш ли време?', accept with 'С удоволствие'. (6) Close with 'Трябва да тръгвам', 'Беше ми приятно', 'Пази се', 'До скоро'. Throughout, remember: Bulgarian has a present 'to be', no infinitive (use 'да' + present), no noun cases, and a suffixed definite article.

examples.russian: Как си? — Добре съм, а ти?
examples.english: How are you? — I'm fine, and you?

examples.russian: Хайде да се видим, имаш ли време?
examples.english: Let's meet up, do you have time?

examples.russian: Трябва да тръгвам. Беше ми приятно!
examples.english: I have to go. It was nice!

reading.title: Цял разговор
reading.text: — Здравей, как си? Не сме се виждали отдавна! — Добре съм, благодаря. Какво ужасно време само! — Да, вали цял ден. Какво ново при теб? — Нищо особено, зает съм с работата. Хайде да пием кафе утре. — С удоволствие! Кога? — В пет? — Звучи добре. Сега трябва да тръгвам. — Беше ми приятно! До скоро, пази се!
reading.translation: — Hi, how are you? We haven't seen each other for a long time! — I'm fine, thanks. What awful weather though! — Yes, it's been raining all day. What's new with you? — Nothing special, I'm busy with work. Let's have a coffee tomorrow. — With pleasure! When? — At five? — Sounds good. Now I have to go. — It was nice! See you soon, take care!

days.speakingPrompt: Role-play a full everyday conversation with a friend you bump into: greet and ask how they are, comment on the weather, catch up on news, invite them for coffee and set a time, then say goodbye politely. Try to use one phrase from each day this week.
